data.title: GPIQ: It Might Be Just What You Need For The Rest Of 2025
data.description:
Introduction

Recently, I have been looking at the market opportunities in some of the well-known technology companies, with stock prices still deep in the red for the year. On the other hand, I don't feel totally comfortable adding more growth exposures as we go into the traditionally slow months of the year. Goldman Sachs Nasdaq-100 Premium Income ETF (NASDAQ:GPIQ) appears to be a better play for me due to its high income distribution and the portfolio strategy that makes it a good growth investment consideration for 2025.

GPIQ overview

GPIQ is Goldman Sachs's solution for growth income derived from writing covered calls off the Nasdaq-100 index portfolio QQQ. On the surface, GPIQ looks very much like many similar income ETFs, including the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ), which I covered early in 2025, and more recently, First Trust Nasdaq BuyWrite Income ETF (FTQI). They may offer a double-digit yield in monthly distribution and a relatively low expense ratio when compared to their CEF peers. Investors may notice small differences in the distribution rates and the expenses when assuming a similar underlying portfolio and consistent income sources (premiums from selling heavily traded covered calls). The following are the top 10 holdings for GPIQ and QQQ, both are heavy in the same top 10, with over 50% weights.

However, the key difference, which could be "hidden" inside the official documents of these ETFs, is the way the ETFs manage the options, including the call strike target and the actual option selling percentage. The ETF performance behavior and performance are dictated by these option trading strategies. GPIQ mainly differs from the other ETFs in managing the selling option coverage (in percentage). In other words, selling 50% covered calls would allow better price appreciation than 100% call selling. That's why GPIQ:

Dynamically sells call options, allowing for participation with rising markets and potential outperformance in negative to flat markets.

As a result, GPIQ could outperform the S&P 500 Index and other larger income ETFs, such as JEPQ, in total returns (a performance chart is given in the later section).

The ETF has gained popularity in a relatively short history since its start on 10/24/2023. In less than two years, GPIQ has accumulated an AUM of over $1 billion, which is impressive considering there are many similar offerings in the market. GPIQ currently has a monthly distribution with a dividend rate of over 10.13% lower than JEPQ's 11.35%, and an expense ratio of 0.29%, which is also lower than JEPQ's 0.35%.

Some key tech components will catch up

The growth-centric QQQ is breaking new highs in recent days. However, many other investors may not be entirely happy as some key technology companies are still some distance away from reaching the previous all-time high achieved early in 2025. Apple Inc. (AAPL) is one of such companies, and its stock is about 15% lower than its 2025 high and about 20% lower than its all-time high accomplished near the end of 2024.

However, the recent technical analysis on AAPL shows a promising price setup (above 50-day and 20-day moving averages), which could lead to a breakout on the upside. Since AAPL has over 7% weight in QQQ (and GPIQ), it would likely help keep the uptrend of QQQ. Similarly, the other heavyweights in QQQ, like Amazon and Google, also have some room to grow and reach the old record highs. These companies together have a huge market cap of $10 trillion, so they would push/keep up the growth sector and GPIQ/QQQ in a very meaningful way.

Earning growth will continue to build on top of the upcoming Q2 reports

Looking at the next tier in the growth space, the current market bull run in the last month seems to hide away the fact that many growth companies are still struggling to recover from the deep price loss incurred in 2025. The following is a partial list I have compiled using the data available in SA. Notice that these companies are the key components of the GPIQ portfolio.

Most of these companies are in negative YTD price performance, and all of them are yet to make the previous highs reached earlier this year. Notice a couple of them have still been down in double digits in 2025.

On the other hand, the forward revenue is projected in the high-growth trajectory as shown in the above table. Many are growing revenue at over 20% for 2025. I would pay close attention to these companies in the coming earnings season. Any positive news could trigger a jump in stock price and result in a significant (double-digit) price gain in a relatively short time. I believe these potential opportunities would not require too much help from a specific catalyst or a unique tailwind.

The main takeaway here is that I expect these companies to carry on bullish momentum and drive a decent performance for QPIQ's underlying portfolio in 2025.

GPIQ is a well-balanced play for growth income investors

Looking ahead, the next few months may not be a traditionally strong season for the equity market, including QQQ. As highlighted in the following table, QQQ has not been doing so well in August, September, and October. The 10-year averages are mostly lower than other months, particularly the earlier months of the year.

Given such historical patterns, it might be a better strategy to hold GPIQ instead of QQQ. Investors may remain bullish on the growth stocks for the rest of 2025, but will likely have to face a bumpy road first.

There are two main reasons in favor of GPIQ. The first is that the seasonality of the above QQQ price patterns typically means high volatility; GPIQ has a much lower volatility at the portfolio level. GPIQ's 52-week Historical Volatility is 20.3, and QQQ's is at 22. This means GPIQ will offer more stability.

The second reason is that investors have the flexibility to deploy the high-income distribution monthly. For example, one can use it to buy QQQ if the volatility turns out to be low or acceptable for a certain time. This gradual approach is my favorite one to navigate through the market volatility. Keep in mind that the flexibility also applies to buying shares of other choices of interests, such as small-cap stocks, value plays for market rotation, or just more income-generating holdings, such as reinvesting in GPIQ.

The following is a 1-year total performance comparison for the mentioned growth ETFs. One can see GPIQ is a rather "surprising" winner by gaining the most, 13.37% beating QQQ (13.18%) and S&P 500 SPY (12.35%).

Note that the above time frame was specifically chosen to illustrate the GPIQ's potential to outperform during certain choppy market periods. I think the upcoming months may exhibit the choppiness that could enable GPIQ to do better or reasonably well.

Risk analysis

All the risks pertinent to the growth portfolio of QQQ will be present in GPIQ, although some impacts could be smaller, such as slightly downward market behavior, which may be caused by macro worries, like slowing economic growth, earnings growth deceleration, etc.

However, the main risk is from the events that could cause sharp drawdowns quickly. For example, the recent tariff-related news could send the market to have big drops in the past 3 months or so. I would expect much less dramatic events to break out on the tariff front, but they are still possible in the next few weeks or months. Big drawdowns are typically very difficult and take a long time to recover from for GPIQ.

Closing thoughts

The stock market may take a breather moving forward into the next few months that may not be too market-friendly, historically speaking, but income investors could still hold on to a growth income portfolio such as GPIQ. Some key technology growth names are still in the recovery process, and the overall growth segment is expected to remain resilient. If the coming (Q2) earnings from technology companies would bring more positive "surprises", the stock market could continue its uptrend in the 2nd half of the year. In such a case, QPIQ has a great chance to outperform the S&P 500. I rate GPIQ a buy and recommend it as a solid growth play for 2025.

data.title: SPHD Explained: Is This High-Yielding Dividend ETF Right For You?
data.description:
Introduction

The Invesco S&P 500 High Dividend Low Volatility ETF (NYSEARCA:SPHD) is designed to track the 50 least volatile high-yielding S&P 500 Index stocks. Aimed at investors who prioritize safety and income over growth, SPHD has a 0.30% expense ratio and has amassed $3.16 billion in assets under management since its launch in 2012, making it one of the more recognizable ETFs on the market today. In this article, I will walk you through SPHD's selection process, the types of returns you can expect in bull and bear markets, how reinvesting impacts long-term portfolio income, and compare and contrast its fundamentals against two cap-weighted and equal-weighted S&P 500 Index ETFs. I hope you enjoy the read.

The Invesco S&P 500 High Dividend Low Volatility ETF: An Overview

Product Details and Fast Facts

Consider the following product overview provided by Invesco:

The Invesco S&P 500 High Dividend Low Volatility ETF is based on the S&P 500 Low Volatility High Dividend Index. The Fund will invest at least 90% of its total assets in common stocks that comprise the Index. Standard & Poor's compiles, maintains and calculates the Index, which is composed of 50 securities traded on the S&P 500 Index that historically have provided high dividend yields and low volatility. The Fund and the Index are rebalanced and reconstituted semi-annually, in January and July.

Additional characteristics, as described on SPHD's website, fact sheet, and summary prospectus, are as follows:

  • Fund Ticker: SPHD
  • Index Provider: S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC
  • Exchange: NYSE Arca
  • Inception Date: October 18, 2012
  • Total Expense Ratio: 0.30%
  • Last Price: $48.37
  • 12 Month Distribution Yield: 3.48%
  • Bid/Ask Premium/Discount: -0.04%
  • Holdings (Excluding Cash): 50
  • Average Trading Volume (30 Days): 519,944
  • Shares Outstanding: 66.47M
  • Assets Under Management: $3.16B
  • Weighting Scheme: Yield-Weighted
  • Marginable: Yes
  • Short Selling: Yes
  • Options: Yes

SPHD Index Methodology

As noted in the graphic below, the S&P 500 Low Volatility High Dividend Index "selects the 50 least volatile stocks (measured by trailing 252-day standard deviation) from the 75 highest yielding stocks of the S&P 500." The chart to the right shows how SPHD outperformed the S&P 500 Index in 8/11 of the largest drawdowns since its inception, and this captures the essence of what SPHD tries to accomplish. It's designed for investors averse to risk and who desire an above-average income.

There are a couple of key points I want to highlight. First, the number of stocks per GICS sector is capped at 10 at each reconstitution after the last business day in January and July and at each rebalancing after the last business day of December and June. Second, dividend yield is the driving factor, not low volatility. It's used in the initial step to bring the number of stocks from 500 to 75, and again in the last step, when Index components are weighted by their trailing dividend yields.

To help put all these steps together, I created a graph showing how the S&P 500 Index breaks down when you filter for the top 300, 225, 150, and 75 stocks by dividend yield. I also calculated each stock's 252-day trailing volatility and provided the median figure for all S&P 500 stocks by sector, so you can get a feel for how that last volatility screen impacts the Index.

The bottom numbers show that there are currently 17 Real Estate securities within the top 75 by yield, followed by 14 and 11 for Consumer Staples and Financials. However, the 10 security per sector rule applies, so next in line are Utilities, Energy, and Health Care, which have 24, 15, and 12 securities within the top 150. That 150 line is a reasonable cutoff for the foreseeable future, and it points to the Index always favoring these sectors. Not surprisingly, these were also SPHD's top six sectors as of June 27, 2025.

Notice how Technology is absent from the sector holdings list, and that will likely be the case moving forward. Only four stocks (HPQ, SWKS, HPE, TXN) rank in the top 150 by yield, and all of them rank outside the top 300 by volatility, which is what disqualified HPQ and SWKS. As for HPE and TXN, they'll need a significant change in their yield and volatility to have a chance. Given how those two statistics often work against each other, it's likely SPHD's tech sector exposure will be limited to only two stocks at most, or less than 3% by weight.

SPHD Performance and Dividends

Annualized Active Return vs. S&P 500 Index ETF

The following table highlights SPHD's annualized active returns against the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV). For the first eight years, the active returns were relatively modest and mostly around the +/- 10% level. However, active returns have grown substantially since the pandemic, and except for 2022, in a negative way. The expansion of big tech really hurt SPHD shareholders from a total returns perspective.

This change shows up nicely on SPHD's three-year rolling returns chart. After trading places with IVV for the first half, SPHD began to underperform in 2018 before bottoming out in March 2020. Performance improved due to its 18.73% beat in 2022, but SPHD's three-year returns have still trailed IVV's for the last seven years or so.

Dividends and Dividend Growth

Although SPHD has underperformed on total returns, it has proven to be a reliable income investment. For example, a person with $10,000 choosing to reinvest all dividends would grow their annual income from $436 to $1,068 between 2013 and 2024, which works out to 8.49% compound annual growth over 11 years. Meanwhile, an IVV shareholder would see their income grow from $235 to $661 over that period, or an annualized 9.86%.

IVV's dividend growth rate is better, but SPHD's higher starting yield allows investors to benefit more from compounding. From the chart, the difference in income was $201 in 2013 ($436 vs. $235), but SPHD's income grew to $1,068 in 2024, a difference of $407.

However, this income growth advantage was not present for investors choosing to withdraw their distributions. As shown below, SPHD's annual income was $195 higher in 2013 ($428 vs. $233) but only $141 higher in 2024 ($675 vs. $534), demonstrating how dividend growth and total returns are closely linked. IVV narrowed the gap due to stronger price appreciation, which increased the portfolio base from which dividends are paid. Consequently, SPHD was less attractive for income investors as the holding period extended.

SPHD Tax Considerations

Readers should also consider asset allocation if they plan to invest in SPHD. Given its favoritism for REITs, not all of the fund's distributions will be considered qualified dividend income, or QDI. You can see SPHD's QDI percentage in the fund's latest Annual Financial Statements, and for the fiscal year ending August 31, 2024, it was 84%.

With 20.36% currently allocated to the Real Estate sector, all of which is to REITs, investors can expect SPHD's QDI to drift closer to 80% in the short term. In addition, with 17 and 24 REITs currently in the top 75 and top 150 by yield, respectively, there's a good chance SPHD's QDI will be in the 80-90% range for the foreseeable future, even if the sector recovers. Therefore, I suggest holding SPHD in a tax-advantaged account like an IRA or a 401 (k).

Fundamentals: SPHD vs. S&P 500 Index ETFs

Big tech's success has also created significant fundamental differences between SPHD and S&P 500 Index ETFs like IVV. As shown below, SPHD's forward P/E ratio is 15.08x, compared to 22.33 for the S&P 500 Index. SPHD also has a much higher dividend yield (5.36% vs. 1.48%), but its growth rates are several percentage points worse across the board.

The chart to the left also confirms SPHD has strong value, dividend, low volatility, and size factor exposure compared to the S&P 500 Index, with offsets in growth, momentum, and quality. Besides performance history, this is a different way to view ETF investing. Readers may consider using SPHD to diversify their portfolios from a factor perspective and cover their bases in case the big tech stocks that lead the S&P 500 Index fall out of favor.

I also created a fundamental statistics table for SPHD's top 25 holdings using data from Seeking Alpha. At the bottom are two comparators: IVV and an equal-weight S&P 500 Index fund, which is essentially the Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight ETF (RSP) with a forced rebalance to 0.20% per stock. While the holdings are bound to change, the factor differences paint a nice picture of what SPHD attempts to accomplish. Have a quick scan, and I'll provide some high-level takeaways afterward.

1. Nearly all of SPHD's top 25 holdings have trailing dividend yields above 3%, and its weighted average dividend yield, otherwise known as the Index yield, is strong at 5.11%. In comparison, the Index yields for IVV and RSP (after a forced rebalance) are only 1.23% and 1.89%.

2. SPHD's weighted average 252-day standard deviation of returns is 23.42% compared to 31.29% and 29.20% for IVV and RSP, respectively. While SPHD and RSP are similar in terms of size factor exposure (weighted average market capture), SPHD does a much better job selecting low-volatility stocks. That's partially attributed to its volatility screen, but it has more to do with the sectors SPHD is designed to favor the most, like Real Estate, Consumer Staples, and Utilities. These sectors are already considered less volatile on average, so in this sense, the Index's volatility screen may be a bit redundant.

3. SPHD trades at 17.92x trailing earnings, which represents a 32% and 17% discount relative to IVV and RSP. SPHD also trades at a 50% discount relative to IVV when using the P/CF ratio, so this is why Morningstar's Factor Profile shows SPHD with extreme value factor exposure. That's also complemented by poor growth factor exposure, evidenced by SPHD's -0.86% three-year historical earnings per share growth rate. Sales growth isn't much better at 3.86%, and that pales in comparison to IVV's 11.07% figure.

4. SPHD's weighted average ROE, ROA, and net margins are 17%, 10%, and 11% lower than IVV's, indicating its components are of lesser quality. That's an important takeaway for all high-yield investors, as deeply discounted high-yield stocks might be trading low for good reasons. It's important to do proper due diligence and be prepared to sacrifice capital appreciation in exchange for a higher income.

Summary and Conclusion

SPHD may be appropriate for conservative equity investors seeking an above-average income. Its current annual distribution rate is 3.51%, paid quarterly, and since yield and price are inversely correlated, it tends to select S&P 500 Index stocks on the lower end of the market cap spectrum. In particular, my deeper analysis of the Index's composition reveals that SPHD will likely favor the Real Estate, Consumer Staples, and Utilities sectors for the foreseeable future, three areas underrepresented in S&P 500 Index ETFs. That means you can add diversification to the list of benefits SPHD offers, alongside lower volatility and higher income.

It may also be useful to view SPHD's advantages from a factor perspective. Its selection process indicates it should consistently do a good job emphasizing the value, dividend, and low volatility factors, while providing minimal exposure to the growth, quality, and, at least temporarily, momentum factors. My own fundamental observations indicate this divide has increased since the pandemic, leading to large return differences. As a result, SPHD shareholders should be prepared for relatively poor total returns in exchange for the higher distributions, which can be accelerated through reinvestment.

This article answers these three main questions about SPHD:

  1. What type of investor is SPHD best suited for?
  2. How can investors utilize SPHD as part of a broader investment strategy?
  3. What types of market conditions does SPHD tend to succeed in?

I hope I have answered these questions to your satisfaction, but if anything is unclear, please feel free to reach out in the comments section below. I should also add that this article was intended to provide a general overview of SPHD and, as such, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF. That said, I am happy to provide my opinion on other funds that interest you and compare SPHD's fundamentals and performance with competing income investments. Thank you for reading, and I look forward to the discussion below.

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for education purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: Allegion: Continued M&A Efforts Add To Its Track Record
data.description:
Towards the end of 2024, I concluded that I was not locked into shares of Allegion (NYSE:ALLE), despite a well-timed purchase of the security assets of Stanley Black & Decker (SWK) back in 2022, followed by bolt-on acquisitions made in the meantime.

Trading at a reasonable high-teens earnings multiple, amidst a reasonable amount of debt taken on, I believed that shares traded at largely fair valuations, with these being fair valuations, not necessary being appealing valuations.

In the meantime, shares have been trading flat amidst continued bolt-on M&A efforts which add to the track record here, as shares look largely fairly valued here, as I am growing more impressed with the business here.

Security Products

Allegion is a pure-play provider of security and access solutions, with the business having a mission to create a safer and more accessible world. The company employs over 12,000 employees which develop, manufacture and sell over 30 trusted brands, active across 25 countries, with the business supported by over a thousand patents. Brands include names like AXA, Bricard, Kryptonite, Schlage, and others.

The company generated nearly $4 billion in sales in 2024, some $3.8 billion to be more precise, accompanied by steep EBITDA margins equal to nearly 25% of sales. Some two-thirds of sales are generated from mechanical solutions, about a quarter from electronic security solutions, with software & services making up the remaining 7% of sales. The business is tied to the Americas in which 80% of sales are generated, with 20% of sales derived from various international markets, which are lagging substantially in terms of margins.

The company targets impressive 7% revenue growth across the cycle, composed out of an estimated 3% M&A contribution and the remainder being the outcome of volume and pricing. Typical products to think of include door locks, e-locks, touchless actuators, closers, automatic doors, frames, hinges, etc. Notably on the electronic front, Allegion is growing its exposure, driven by M&A efforts.

The company has come a long way, having grown the business from $2.1 billion in 2015 to $3.8 billion in 2024, with revenues having nearly doubled over this period of time. Growth, to an important extent, was driven by a $900 million deal to acquire the Access Technology business from Stanley Black & Decker, adding $340 million in sales.

Operating margins near 20% have risen a bit to levels in the low-twenties with the passage of time, driven by sale and efficiency advancements, as the company announced substantial growth while buying back about one in every ten shares as well.

All this has been reflected in the share price, which has gradually risen from levels around the $60 mark in 2015 to hit a high around $150 in 2021. Ever since, shares have largely traded in a $100-$150 range, now trading at the higher end of the range.

Valuation Thoughts

For the year 2023, the company posted adjusted earnings of $6.69 per share on $3.65 billion in sales. The company guided for sales up around 2.5%, with adjusted earnings seen moving up to $7.00-$7.15 per share.

Through 2024 the company announced a couple of bolt-on transactions, as growth was not necessarily convincing. Trading around the $140 mark in December, the 88 million shares of the company were valued at $12.2 billion, this excluding $1.5 billion in net debt, for a $13.7 billion enterprise valuation.

The business was on track to post earnings close to $7.40 per share, for an 18-19 times adjusted earnings multiple, as net debt of $1.52 billion resulted in a 1.7 times leverage ratio based on EBITDA seen around $900 million. With shares having enjoyed quite some momentum in the fall, this made me require seeing a dip before getting upbeat on the shares.

Trading Flat

Since the end of 2024, shares of Allegion have traded in a $120-$150 range, with the lows induced by the tariff discussion, as the company has made a full recovery ever since.

So far this year, the company announced numerous bolt-on deals as the company announced a solid 6% increase in the quarterly dividend payable at $2.04 per share, with dividends now hiked for 11 years in a row.

In February, the company announced a 3% increase in full-year sales to $3.77 billion, with adjusted earnings up over 8% to $7.53 per share, this being quite a clean earnings number. Net debt of $1.50 billion was perfectly manageable, with EBITDA reported at $899 million for the year. The company guided for a modest 1-3% reported sales growth in 2025, driven by organic revenue growth, with adjusted earnings seen up modestly to $7.75 per share, plus or minus ten cents.

Following a strong first quarter, in which sales were up more than 5% the company maintained the guidance, with net debt flattish around $1.50 billion, as the company sees upside to the revenue guidance than to tariffs, but for now, expects a flattish and unchanged guidance with regard to its earnings.

Early in June, the company announced a more substantial deal, as the company announced an EUR 330 million deal to acquire German-based ELATEC GmbH. This firm is specialized in RFID credentials and reader solutions with an EUR 60-65 million sales contribution seen in 2026, for a 5.0-5.5 times forward sales multiple. The deal will be accretive to earnings that year, the extent of which is not specified, which is comforting as the business should be solidly profitable in that case, with the transaction already closing early in July.

With 87 million shares trading at $148, the market value comes in at $12.9 billion, for a $14.4 billion enterprise valuation. This results in a 3.8 times sales multiple awarded to Allegion, with the deal coming at a premium, adding about nearly 2% to total sales.

What Now?

The deal comes at quite an expense, as the deal comes in at nearly $400 million based on prevailing exchange rates, pushing up net debt towards $1.9 billion. With EBITDA reported at $900 million in 2024, and modest growth seen this year, leverage ratios are seen around 2 times.

Based on earnings power seen around $7.75 per share, after first quarter earnings were up thirty-one cents on the year before already, I see upside to the earnings guidance. Shares now trade at 19 times earnings, these being clean adjusted earnings, still resulting in a similar story as last December.

That said, I am growing more appreciative of the bolt-on M&A growth story of the business, certainly amidst modest leverage employed, and reasonable valuations after all. This makes me conclude to have continued interest in the shares and business, with a full intention to get involved on dips and covering future M&A events.

data.title: Sandvik: Valuation Is Not Attractive Enough To Justify A Buy
data.description:
Investment Action

I give a hold rating to Sandvik (OTCPK:SDVKY). As much as I believe SDVKY’s powerful growth drivers and margin expansion plans are credible, the current valuation offers only mediocre returns that do not justify a buy at this stage.

Company Description

SDVKY has three primary business areas: Mining and Rock Solutions, its largest segment, where it provides equipment, services, and digital solutions for the mining and infrastructure industries; Machining and Intelligent Manufacturing Solutions, where it provides advanced metal-cutting tools and digital manufacturing software; and Rock Processing Solutions, which supplies equipment for processing rock and minerals. SDVKY has a global footprint in >150 countries and a workforce of 41,000, making it a major player in the industrial sector.

Mining Powerhouse Supports Growth

The bulk of SDVKY's growth story is anchored by its Mining and Rock Solutions segment, which is the core profit and cash engine. For a quick background, this segment has delivered an 11% revenue CAGR from FY19 to FY24, scaling into a SEK65 billion business with a solid adj. EBITA margin of 20.4%. Momentum remains strong, as 1Q25 saw solid organic order intake, double-digit y/y growth in equipment, and 5% y/y growth in the higher-margin aftermarket business. Margin expansion momentum is also clear, with 1Q25 adj. EBITA margin expanding to 20.8% from 18.2% in 1Q24.

Looking ahead, I believe the growth outlook looks compelling. At its Capital Markets Day, management guided for this segment to deliver an 8% revenue CAGR through 2030, scaling the segment into SEK100 billion revenue. This growth is plausible as it is driven by powerful secular drivers tied to the global energy transition, which is structurally increasing demand for critical commodities like copper, lithium, and rare earths.

There are three industry challenges that are directly driving demand for SDVKY’s solutions. First, a structural supply deficit for key metals is emerging, with copper alone facing a projected 24% supply gap versus demand by 2035. This dynamic is real and is forcing miners to accelerate both greenfield and brownfield investments, and to do so, miners need more equipment, and this drives demand for SDVKY’s equipment. Second, ore grades are declining steadily and are expected to drop by 30.1% for copper from 2000 to 2030. This means that miners must process significantly more material to yield the same output, and this increases the need for high-productivity and high-efficiency equipment to offset these lower yields. Third, the industry faces a severe skilled labor shortage, as seen from the 38% decline in mining engineering graduates between 2014 and 2022. This dynamic forces miners to turn to automation and autonomous technologies to maintain productivity.

SDVKY is in a very good position to capitalize on these trends as it holds the #1 market position in electrification, automation, and digitalization, and as I discussed above, these are precisely the solutions miners need to address the industry challenges. There are already tangible figures to support this view. SDVKY’s Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV) portfolio, for example, saw its installed base grow by 30% from FY23 to FY24, clearly demonstrating customer demand for equipment that improves productivity. For background info: The advantage of battery-powered equipment is that it is not tethered to a power cable during operation. This allows for greater operational flexibility, faster setup times, and the ability to complete entire work cycles without interruption for refueling or recharging mid-shift. Basically, it maximizes the equipment's uptime and overall output. Another example is the AutoMine autonomous solutions. This includes SDVKY’s autonomous mining vehicles, such as loaders and trucks, and they are already seeing robust demand as customers seek to offset labor shortages.

Business Revenue Has Become More Resilient

SDVKY's business has also become a lot more resilient than in the past. As a quick history recap, SDVKY has increased its share of high-margin aftermarket and recurring software revenues from 31% of total sales in FY19 to 44% in FY24. This success is particularly clear within the Mining and Rock segment, where the aftermarket now represents 72% of revenue as of 1Q25, up from 58% in 2017. And importantly, management has plans to replicate the same playbook in the rock processing segment, targeting an aftermarket share of 65-70% in the coming years.

There are two key implications here. One is that by deepening customer relationships and embedding its technology and services within customer operations, the relationship becomes stickier. This, in turn, creates a virtuous cycle of higher margin recurring revenue and stable cash flow. Two is that this increased revenue visibility should justify SDVKY trading at a structurally higher multiple than in the past.

Disciplined Execution Driving Profitability

Internally, management also has a solid plan to drive up margins. They have explicitly reaffirmed the group adj. EBITA margin target of 20-22% during the capital market days, and I believe they can achieve it. One of the key steps was the launch of a new restructuring program within the machining segment, targeting an additional SEK1 billion in annual run-rate savings by 2030, on top of SEK2 billion already delivered from prior programs. Perhaps the more important thing is that there is evidence of these initiatives producing results. In 1Q25, SDVKY adj. EBITA margin rose to 19.7% from 18.2% y/y, with SEK307 million in savings directly driving up profitability.

Valuation

The issue with investing in SDVKY is the valuation. I agree with the structural drivers that can drive growth for the business, but even if I were to use management's long-term guidance—7% revenue CAGR and 22% adj. EBITA margin—and attach an 18x forward PE multiple (more aftermarket revenue that makes the revenue visibility stronger and growth more durable), I have the stock trading at SEK349 in FY29 (forward PE values on FY30 numbers), which is a 42% upside over ~4 years. This is not attractive as it yields slight-above-market-like returns annually (~10%). Either revenue growth must accelerate a lot stronger than expected, or multiples have to go way higher. For the former, it is possible, but I don’t think the market will price in that since management guided for 7% revenue CAGR. For the latter, 18x is already a premium, as it is the +1 standard deviation over the past 10 years, and SDVKY has historically traded within a range of ~12x to 18x.

Risk

The machining segment is still exposed to the low-growth cutting tools market and cyclical industrial demand, so this could be a drag on revenue growth in the near term. The new SEK1 billion savings program in this segment, while promising, comes with a high SEK3 billion implementation cost, so near-term margins could see a dip, and if there are disruptions during the transition period, it may even hurt operations, putting more pressure on margins and growth. Additionally, FX is a major headwind, as management guided to a -SEK600 million impact in 2Q25.

Conclusion

While SDVKY's mining segment growth, an increasing mix of higher-margin recurring revenue, and disciplined execution support a strong structural growth story, the stock valuation is not attractive. By my math, it only provides ~10% returns annually. As such, I am maintaining a hold rating until either growth accelerates beyond the current 7% CAGR guidance or the valuation resets to offer a more attractive risk-reward profile.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Will Realty Income Cut Its Dividend?
data.description:
Over the past year, I have written a few articles that were a bit critical of Realty Income (NYSE:O), and this prompted some of you to ask me whether I think that Realty Income's dividend is still safe or not.

It is a fair concern.

Realty Income's portfolio is suffering growing tenant issues, it faces significant debt maturities in the coming years, and interest rates have surged to much higher levels. Moreover, we could also be approaching a recession, and trade wars could further impact the health of some of its biggest tenants.

These growing risks are well reflected in the company's rising dividend yield. Investors are asking for higher risk premiums for owning the stock:

Is Realty Income at risk of cutting its dividend?

My answer to this is a clear and resounding no.

Realty Income has its issues, but its dividend is still perfectly safe in my opinion, and in today's article, I am going to give 5 reasons why a cut is very unlikely:

Reason #1: Track Record

Let's start with the obvious here.

Realty Income has a 30-year track record of steady dividend growth. This time period includes the great financial crisis and the pandemic, which were some of the worst possible crises for real estate investors.

The pandemic was arguably the worst possible crisis for a net lease REIT (NETL) because its tenants couldn't even operate their properties for a little while.

Even then, it still managed to not only maintain but even hike its dividend in 2020, 2021, and 2022 - all while many other REITs (VNQ) were forced to cut their dividend.

Could an upcoming recession, coupled with a trade war, hurt some of Realty Income's tenants?

Sure, it could. But the pain would be nowhere near what these same tenants went through in 2020.

The pandemic experience should give investors a lot of encouragement concerning Realty Income's dividend safety. This is especially true concerning the following point:

Reason #2: Historically Low Leverage

Realty Income, like most other REITs, spent a good chunk of the past decade deleveraging its balance sheet.

It has grown mostly by raising equity rather than debt, and as a result, its leverage ratio is today just 35%.

Most of that has a fixed rate and many years left till maturity.

Therefore, Realty Income won't be that significantly impacted by the surge in interest rates.

This is not to say that Realty Income is not impacted. It sure is. But the impact is manageable, especially considering that the REIT enjoys organic growth with ~1.5% annual rent escalations in its leases, and it retains about 30% of its cash flow to keep acquiring more properties.

Therefore, Realty Income should be just fine, even if we remain in a higher-for-longer environment. It came into this rate-hiking cycle with a conservative balance sheet that has earned it an A-credit rating.

Reason #3: Better Diversified Than Ever

We have expressed our concerns about Realty Income's average portfolio quality degrading in recent years. Moreover, we have also criticized Realty Income for losing its focus and investing all over the place.

We stand by these concerns.

However, it should also be noted that its portfolio is bigger and better diversified than ever, which in itself also mitigates risks.

As an example, it now generates about 15% of its rental income from Europe, and therefore, it is not just at the mercy of the Fed in the US.

Also, while we are worried about some of its top tenants like Walgreens (WBA), those individual tenants only represent a few percentage points of its rental income.

Being very well diversified is a great risk mitigator in today's uncertain world.

Reason #4: Room for Error

If things get tough, Realty Income won't be pressed to immediately cut its dividend because it has a lot of liquidity, low leverage, well-staggered maturities, and a low dividend payout ratio.

All of this gives it room for error.

Even if, say, its 5 biggest tenants stopped paying rent, Realty Income would still cover its dividend.

If it then had to repossess all of those assets, redevelop them, and release them, that would be very expensive, but since it has relatively little leverage and lots of available liquidity, it could just temporarily tap into its credit facility to fund those projects.

It could overpay its dividend for quite a while before the actual health of the REIT would be at risk.

The management has run the business in a way that never puts it under pressure to cut the dividend. Their entire reputation is based on their ability to pay a safe and growing dividend, and they don't want to risk ruining that.

Reason #5: Amazing Management

Any REIT can do well over the short run if it owns good assets that enjoy strong tailwinds.

But it takes a special team to succeed decade after decade, crisis after crisis, and Realty Income has that special team.

They are not focused on maximizing gains in any given year or bull market. They are focused on maximizing value over a full cycle, paying close attention to the next downturn and how they will get through it without having to cut the dividend.

To give you an example of this: Realty Income was one of the first net lease REITs to aggressively exit the net lease office sector by spinning its assets into a separate REIT at a time when those assets still commanded a decent valuation. It allowed them to exit the sector and strengthen their portfolio without having to cut their dividend. Other REITs like W. P. Carey (WPC) were not so forthcoming and ended up having to cut their dividend because they were late to take action.

Thinking ahead of the crowd will allow Realty Income to avoid major setbacks that could cost it its dividend.

Closing Note

I am very confident that Realty Income will manage to keep growing its dividend for decades to come.

We are not buying at High Yield Landlord because I think that some of its close peers are even more attractive, but I am certainly not bearish and wouldn't bet on a dividend cut.

The company has faced major crises before and has been just fine. Today, it is bigger and better diversified than ever, so I am really not concerned about it.

data.title: Teladoc Health: A Beaten-Down Bargain Worth A Second Look (Upgrade)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Teladoc Health (TDOC) is not an easy investment to make. The appeal here is that this stock has been completely beaten down and is priced at just 7x next year's free cash flow.

However, the issue the business faces is that there's just too little topline growth. And without the growth in its engine to drive the business forward, tackling its debt profile is a problem.

So, I believe that there's enough here for me to upgrade this stock to tepid buy, having been neutral on it for the past few months. But I'm also rather clear about the risks.

Rapid Recap

As I discussed back in February 2025, Teladoc was a Deep Value Returns recommendation, where investors made 40%+ in 3 months, see below.

Looking back now, my timing looks fortuitous. Maybe. Probably. But does it matter?

I had a prepared mind going in. And recognized when the risk-reward had changed away from me and my subscribers. So, we sold. And kept neutral since.

Teladoc's Near-Term Prospects

Teladoc is the default virtual healthcare provider.

Whether it’s seeing a doctor online or managing chronic conditions, they’ve built a platform that makes care more accessible.

One of their biggest success stories is BetterHelp, which is now the largest direct-to-consumer virtual therapy platform in the world. Over a million people used it in 2024, and nearly half were brand new to therapy.

Plus, it’s fast (most people get matched with a therapist in under 48 hours), and it’s flexible—either paid directly or now increasingly covered by insurance, thanks to the recent acquisition of UpLift, which already works with health plans covering over 100 million lives. That opens the door for more people to get care.

That being said, Teladoc isn’t alone. In mental health, they’re up against other virtual care startups like Talkspace (TALK), as well as, traditional health systems ramping up their own virtual offerings. So, it's not like Teladoc is ever going to operate in a winner-takes-all sector.

Given this context, let's now discuss its fundamentals.

Revenue Growth Rates Could Stabilize

The big question for investors is simple: Can Teladoc find sustainable growth again? Because if investors can’t get comfortable with Teladoc’s “return to growth” story, then—no matter how cheap the stock looks—it’ll likely keep getting cheaper.

There’s some reason for near-term optimism. Teladoc’s second half of 2025 is set up to show growth, largely because it’s coming off easy comps—H2 2024 saw two straight quarters of -3% y/y revenue. But that’s the low-hanging fruit.

What really matters is whether Teladoc can get back to something like 5% top-line growth in 2026. Without that, I suspect most investors will view the stock as too speculative—even if the valuation looks attractive.

TDOC Stock Valuation — 7x Forward Free Cash Flow

Teladoc is currently trading at just 7x forward free cash flow. On paper, that looks cheap. But there are a few caveats.

For starters, the company still has around $350 million of net debt. That’s not fatal—but it’s a blemish investors can’t ignore. Personally, I prefer companies with a net cash position of at least 5% of their market cap—it gives the thesis a second layer of margin of safety.

In Teladoc’s case, we’re not only facing uncertain growth prospects, but also a balance sheet that could quickly become a problem if anything goes wrong. Debt doesn’t matter—until suddenly, it does.

Furthermore, at the start of the year, Teladoc was guiding for up to $220 million of free cash flow.

But that changed.

In Q1 2025, Teladoc lowered its guidance to about $190 million. As a result, I now think $205–210 million in 2026 is probably the best investors can realistically hope for.

That brings us back to valuation.

The stock is priced at 7x forward free cash flow. This means that Teladoc trades at an adjusted PEG ratio of around 3.5x (7x FCF divided by ~2% expected revenue growth). That’s not screaming "cheap," even with the low multiple.

As a reference point, I'll consider businesses with a PEG ratio below 3x to be compelling. But I have made exceptions from time to time.

Risk Factors

The biggest risk? There’s no clear growth driver.

Yes, Teladoc has a strong narrative. Yes, there's a relative new CEO at Teladoc. And yes, Chuck Divita, the new CEO, argues that aggressive changes are being made. But as Warren Buffett once said:

When a management team with a reputation for brilliance tackles a business with a reputation for poor economics, it is the reputation of the business that remains intact.

The second risk is the debt. Until Teladoc meaningfully reduces its debt load, the market won’t reward it with a higher multiple.

That said, if Teladoc can generate around $200 million in free cash flow over the next 12 months and use that to pay down debt, the company could reach a net neutral balance sheet—where cash and debt cancel each other out. That would be a meaningful step forward, where I could see the stock getting re-rated higher.

The Bottom Line

I’m upgrading Teladoc from a hold to a tepid buy. Why? Because at 7x forward free cash flow, the stock is undeniably cheap on paper.

The setup for H2 2025 looks decent, thanks to easier comps, and if they can hit $200 million+ in free cash flow while chipping away at the debt, that changes the story.

Sure, the growth outlook is still murky, and the balance sheet isn’t exactly bulletproof.

But there’s just enough here—enough cash flow, enough potential debt progress, and enough strategic upside with BetterHelp and UpLift—that I think the risk/reward is starting to lean in investors' favor again.

I'm not pounding the table... but I’m definitely more interested again.

data.title: NMCO: Buying Opportunity Has Passed, CEF Gets Another Sell Rating
data.description:
Introduction

What the IRS tax brackets will look like after 2025 is still in the hands of Congress, with the GOP House and GOP Senate not on the same page for the final version of the “Big Beautiful Bill.” Today’s brackets revert back to 2016, inflation adjusted, if a new law isn’t passed. All of this matters for municipal bondholders as interest isn’t subject to federal taxes and one’s marginal tax bracket rate affects how they are evaluated against ed taxable fixed income assets, like corporate bonds.

I last reviewed the Nuveen Municipal Credit Opps Fund (NYSE:NMCO) in the fall of 2023 (article link), giving it a rare Strong Sell rating as interest rate hikes were destroying its NAV, and pulling the price down with. For reasons explained here later, while up, I’m still giving NMCO a Sell rating.

Nuveen Municipal Credit Opps Fund review

Seeking Alpha describes this CEF as:

The Fund’s primary investment objective is to provide a high level of current income exempt from regular U.S. federal income tax. The Fund’s secondary investment objective is to seek total return. Under normal circumstances, the Fund will invest at least 80% of its Assets in municipal securities, the income from which is exempt from regular U.S. federal income taxes. Benchmark: S&P Municipal Yield TR USD

This CEF has Common Assets of $594MM, with a Total Exposure of $1B thanks to its 42% leverage ratio. Nuveen provides fees at two levels.

Since NMCO uses leverage, performance is affected in two ways as rates move up and down as yield and leverage costs change.

Nuveen adds to the above description with this:

The Fund invests primarily in high yielding, low- to medium-quality municipal securities that, at the time of investment, are rated Baa/BBB or lower or, if unrated, are judged by the portfolio managers to be of comparable quality. No more than 30% of the Fund's managed assets will be in municipal securities rated CCC+/Caa1 or lower at the time of investment or unrated but judged to be of comparable quality. No more than 10% of the Fund's managed assets can be in defaulted securities or securities of issuers in bankruptcy or insolvency proceedings at the time of investment.

Source: nuveen NMCO.

The Fund is scheduled to terminate at the close of business on 10/1/2031. That said, the Fund’s Board of Trustees, without any action by the shareholders of the fund, can extend the Fund’s term for up to two one-year periods.

Holdings review

One thing I like about Nuveen is they provide more data than most funds do about the fund's portfolio.

Even excluding zero-coupon bonds, the average bond price of $85 leaves a lot of upsides if bonds are upgraded or interest rates decline. for those worried about the AMT process, 20% of the income from NMCO is exempt from those calculations. Starting with sectors, we see that almost 32% comes from the two tax-oriented sectors, with nice diversification otherwise.

With 63% of the bonds not rated, it limits the value of the next table. While most funds of this type usually have a high percentage not rated, over 60% stands out.

That said, with over 40% of the rated bonds being below investment-grade, the state of the economy comes into play more so than a fund invested mostly in investment-grade bonds, especially with over 7% rated B or lower where defaults become way too common.

Next, we see the fund is well diversified across the states, keeping in mind the percentages reflect where the issues is located, not that the state itself was the issuer.

Next we see that only a small percent of the portfolio matures over the next four years, though 90% of the portfolio is eligible to be Called over that time period. The above data table shows a long effective duration of 16+ years, with the WAM being 21 years, typical of municipal bond funds.

With the current estimated yield on BB-rated bonds, the low maturity rate is a positive as I estimate the majority of the coupons are over 5.5% of those maturing soon.

Top holdings

When compiling the data by Obligator, we see Puerto Rico is the biggest one, with the Top 10 accounting for 27% of the portfolio.

Distributions review

Last June, Nuveen did an across-the-board distribution hike on many of their CEFs with the hope the higher payout would narrow the discount the funds were experiencing. If that happened, it will be shown later. What it did do was change the sources needed to maintain the higher payout, which now is only covered by income at a 63% rate recently.

Over the past fiscal year, ROC provided 31% of the money paid to investors, with the most recent payment requiring 19% ROC.

Price and NAV review

After its post COVID recovery, NMCO has not done very well, though it started too when the FOMC started cutting rates and that would be expected. The next chart shows how the discount (or premium) has moved since inception.

The current 2% discount is less than normal, and this is reflected in that NMCO had an average discount of 5% over the past five years. As for the payout bump-up closing the discount, like other Nuveen CEFs, it did into the fall only to widen again after that.

CEF comparison

Here I included the other Nuveen CEFs also labeled as National High Yield.

  • Nuveen Municipal High Income Opportunity Fund (NMZ)
  • Nuveen Municipal Income (NMI).

NMCO has a consistent history of underperforming compared to these other similar funds, with it being the best only at the 5-year interval. Even worst, those poor results also inflicted investors with the highest StdDev! Based on the small discount currently available and its poor relative returns, I gave NMCO a Sell rating, though I thought about leaving it as a Strong Sell still.

Portfolio strategy

This importance of marginal tax rates hit home this year when filing my dad’s Trust under its EIN, not my dad’s SSN for the first time. Besides losing the joint table benefits, the Trust rate of 22% jumped to 37% and at a much lower level. While it was my mistake, it would have been expected that his CPA would have warned me. One result is I upped his municipal bond exposure to 17% of the portfolio. I’m preparing an article outlining the other changes I made, moving the overall strategy away from income generation to more growth oriented. With the account having a large capital loss carry forward, any trade would be “tax free.”

The main point is that one needs to be diligent to not only changes in the income tax laws, both federal and their state’s, but how a change in filing status might require a strategy change to improve the account’s after-tax results.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Headline Roulette Is A Game: Equity Investing Is Not
data.description:
By Nelson Yu

Transcript

Don’t play headline roulette. Those headlines have been whiplashing us back and forth between positive and negative sentiment. While it’s impossible to ignore these headlines, it’s important for a long-term investor to really think about what matters.

What really matters is understanding, well, where are the sources of underlying company growth? It’s never a good idea to sell at a moment when your emotions are high and you’re panicking. If you sold in April, you would’ve missed out on a 20% rally since that time. We started off the year with this consensus view of US exceptionalism. Nothing was going to stop the US equity market. It was the land for technology. It was going to be protected from any tariff announcement, and the markets were just going to go up. Small-caps were on a tear. You come to today, and now the question is, is US exceptionalism dead? And that was really driven by just some weakness in the dollar and international equity markets outperforming the US equity markets.

While we shouldn’t be driven by conventional wisdom, we need to engage with it. The headlines do tell us about important risks that we need to focus on. Recently, the escalation of risks in the Middle East reminds us how important geopolitical risks can be. The real challenge that investors face in terms of their overall wealth creation is the fact that growth is getting scarce. You’ve got inflation and you’ve got a concentration in the equity markets. So in order to invest for the long term, you need to think about what are the longer-term opportunities I have. You have a massive revolution in productivity that could happen because of AI. There are many beneficiaries that haven’t shown up yet. You’ve got an energy transformation that still needs to happen to power that AI. And finally, with deglobalization, we need to reinvest in our supply chain and our manufacturing onshore. There’s so many investment opportunities that can happen over the long term that investors can profit from.

Have a disciplined strategy, stick to it. We need to find companies with the strategy to win, whether that’s looking across competitive moats, or if your question is on US exceptionalism. There’s plenty of US companies with the right to win. However, you also have to think about Europe, Japan [and] emerging markets. There are also plenty of companies out there that can generate very high returns. And as a matter of fact, you can use deglobalization as your ally by reducing volatility.

And finally, defensive equity strategies [are] so important in this environment. Companies with stable earnings streams, thinking about the ups and downs and how that can affect your portfolio is a really important part of your long-term strategy. If investors just keep following headlines, they won’t be able to access these critical areas of growth that will generate return opportunities for the future.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations, and do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio-management teams, and are subject to change over time.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Fidus Investment: High Yield, Special Dividends And NAV Upside
data.description:
Business development company Fidus Investment Corporation (NASDAQ:FDUS) is selling for a moderate 5% premium to net asset value while covering its base dividend with net investment income and growing its net originations.

The business development company is primarily oriented toward the origination of First Liens and has profited from a seismic shift toward higher quality loans in the last couple of years. Fidus Investment's gross and net origination trends are bullish and indicate potential for organic net investment income growth.

My last stock classification on Fidus Investment was 'Strong Buy' as I viewed the business development company's dividend as being well-covered by net investment income.

In the last quarter, Fidus Investment paid a $0.11 per share extra dividend which supplemented the company's $0.43 per share per quarter base dividend.

Fidus Investment has special dividend potential, thanks to its excess coverage, and I think that the BDC could re-rate into a higher NAV multiple moving forward.

High-Grading Portfolio And Growing Originations Underline Core Investment Proposition

Fidus Investment is a floating-rate business development company with a focus on First Lien origination in the U.S. middle market segment.

As of March 31, 2025, Fidus Investment had an investment portfolio valued at $1.15 billion, up 6% QoQ, thanks to robust demand for new loans. The debt portfolio consisted overwhelmingly of First Liens with a remainder spread out over other investments, including Second Liens, Subordinated Debt and Equity.

Fidus Investment has instituted a significant change in the structure of its debt investment portfolio in recent years by accentuating new originations in First Lien debt instead of Second Lien debt. As a consequence, investors have enjoyed a substantial high-grading in portfolio quality for the business development company, particularly since 2020. As of today, about 79% of Fidus Investment's debt is high-quality First Lien debt.

Fidus Investment's net origination improved considerably in the March-quarter, thanks to high gross originations, but also because of a level of loan repayments that was less than half of what the business development company received in the prior quarter. In the last four quarters, Fidus Investment's profited from $22.6 million in average net originations which compares to $58.3 million in 1Q25.

Thanks to higher net originations (which were positive in five of the last six quarters), Fidus Investment's net investment income rose 3% YoY to $18.2 million. Many BDCs reported falling net investment income in recent quarters, as well sliding portfolio values, as portfolio companies accelerated their loan repayments in a high-rate environment.

Fidus Investment's net investment income easily covers the present dividend and with net originations being positive, we should be able to see growing NII in the next couple of quarters as well.

Fidus Investment's credit quality is also looking very compelling, with the BDC's non-accrual ratio of 0.8% clocking in below the BDC peer group average of 1.1% as of March 31, 2025.

Non-accruals are an important KPI to watch as an increase in non-accruals almost always foreshadows increasing NAV risks (due to BDCs being forced to write down the carrying value of their loans) and higher dividend risks as well.

Well-Covered Base Dividend, Excess Coverage Drives Special Dividend Potential

Robust and consistent excess dividend coverage over time is of utmost importance when it comes to choosing business development companies.

Fidus Investment offers this: The BDC earned $0.53 per share in net investment income in 1Q25 which compares to a total dividend pay-out of $0.54 per share (reflecting a pay-out ratio of 102%).

Just with regard to the base dividend of $0.43 per share, however, the dividend pay-out ratio was just 81% and thus on about the same level as the LTM dividend pay-out ratio. Low variability in the LTM dividend pay-out ratio compared to a BDC' quarterly dividend pay-out ratios indicate a high level of net investment income quality.

Moderate 5% Premium To NAV

Fidus Investment is selling for a moderate premium of 5% to net asset value as of the time of writing this article. This 5% premium is based on the company's last reported net asset value of $19.39 per share as of March 31, 2025 and might undervalue the BDC's solid 80%, particularly because the effective dividend yield could be much higher.

In 1Q25, Fidus Investment paid a total of $0.54 in dividends which included the $0.43 per share base dividend per quarter plus an extra dividend of $0.11 per share to distribute portfolio income exceeding the BDC's net investment income.

The implied net asset value premium places Fidus Investment only slightly above the BDC peer average Price-To-NAV ratio of 1.03x. In my view, Fidus Investment could sell for 1.10x net asset value as the base dividend is well-covered by NII and the BDC has a non-accrual ratio comparable to that of BDC champion Ares Capital Corp. (ARCC). Thus, an intrinsic value around $21.32 per share could be deduced vs. a present stock price of $20.36.

The two most expensive business developing companies are Ares Capital and Blackstone Secured Lending Fund (BSXL) which are selling at 11% to 12% premiums to their respective net asset values. FS KKR Capital Corp. (FSK) has an above average non-accrual ratio of 2.1% and therefore has below-average credit quality in its debt investment portfolio. This is the reason why the BDC is selling for the lowest Price-TO-NAV ratio of 0.9x, reflecting a 10% discount from the last reported net asset value.

Potential Concerns About A Long Position In Fidus Investment

Fidus Investment is fundamentally a floating-rate business development company. As of March 31, 2025, Fidus Investment had 72.8% exposure to floating-rate loans in its debt investment portfolio, so if the central bank were to respond in the latter half of the year to moderating inflation by lowering interest rates, the business development company might see lower portfolio income and a diminishing margin of safety as far as its dividend is concerned.

My Takeaway

Fidus Investment covered its base dividend well with net investment income in 1Q25 and so far, the central bank has proven to be quite reluctant to lower interest rates, which works to the benefit of floating-rate business development companies. Passive income investors therefore continue to have a strong reason to own the floating-rate BDC to produce income.

Moreover, the BDC is growing its net originations which is going to add portfolio incrementally moving forward.

With the base dividend of $0.43 per share per quarter being well-covered by net investment income and the portfolio growing, I think that the present rate environment allows for upside in Fidus Investment's valuation multiple. Strong Buy.

data.title: Affordability Of U.S. New Homes Worsened In May 2025
data.description:
The United States' new home affordability crisis worsened slightly in May 2025, as a higher median new home sale price combined with an increase in the nation's average mortgage rate.

The negative development means the monthly mortgage payment for the median new home sold in the United States once again consumes more than 36% of the pre-tax income of a household earning the median household income. May 2025 represents the 38th consecutive month in which the typical new home sold in the U.S. has been beyond the affordable grasp of the typical American household.

Here are the numbers being that assessment for May 2025. The initial estimate of the median sale price of a new home sold in the U.S. during the month is $426,600. Meanwhile, the 30-year conventional fixed-rate mortgage carried an average interest rate of 6.82% during the month. For a household taking out a mortgage with a zero-down payment [1], the principal and interest portion of their mortgage payment would total $2,423.

That mortgage payment would consume about 40.1% of the monthly income earned by a household at the exact middle of the U.S. income distribution in May 2025. As such, it exceeds the upper threshold of affordability that lenders have historically used to determine whether they will loan money to a household that has no other debt. For households that do carry other debt, lenders prefer their monthly mortgage payments consume no more than 28% of their pre-tax household income. Mortgage borrowers have more financial resilience to deal with unexpected expenses when their mortgage payments fall below these affordability thresholds.

The latest update of our chart tracks the changing relative affordability of the typical new home sold in the U.S. is for the typical American household with respect to the mortgage lending industry's key affordability thresholds from January 2000 through May 2025.

Looking ahead to June 2025, the interest rate for the average 30-year conventional fixed-rate mortgage held steady at 6.82%, which means borrowing costs remain elevated. With mortgage rates stuck, for the affordability of new homes to improve, one or more of the following two things would need to happen:

  • A decline in the price of the median new home sold in the U.S.
  • A significant increase in the income earned by the median household.

About the New Home Affordability Crisis

The affordability crisis for new homes has its origin in the high inflation that was unleashed in March 2021. Although it rose slowly at first, the cost of monthly mortgage payment began to skyrocket after December 2021. As a percentage of median household income, the monthly mortgage payment for a new home climbed above the key 36% threshold of relative affordability in April 2022, remaining above it in every month since.

Notes

[1] We use the zero-down payment scenario because it provides a simple way to account for the opportunity costs of paying a down payment when buying a new home for many homebuyers. Meanwhile, there are several lending programs for qualified homebuyers that do provide a zero-down payment option to try to make buying a home more affordable.

References

U.S. Census Bureau. New Residential Sales Historical Data. Houses Sold. [Excel Spreadsheet]. Accessed 25 June 2025.

U.S. Census Bureau. New Residential Sales Historical Data. Median and Average Sale Price of Houses Sold. [Excel Spreadsheet]. Accessed 25 June 2025.

Freddie Mac. 30-Year Fixed-Rate Mortgages Since 1971. [Online Database]. Accessed 27 July 2025. Note: Starting from December 2022, the estimated monthly mortgage rate is taken as the average of weekly 30-year conventional mortgage rates recorded during the calendar month.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
